channel.title: Anindya Dutta
channel.description: Essays on engineering, leadership, and building things.

item.title: Letting someone go
item.description: The conversation had been six weeks in preparation.

item.title: What Copilot is becoming
item.description: In 2021, when I first told people I was building the AI assistant in Outlook, the response was a polite variation of "interesting, what does it do."

item.title: Samsung, Amazon, Microsoft, Meta, Microsoft again
item.description: The thing nobody tells you about changing companies is that you keep arriving as a beginner.

item.title: Why I came back
item.description: The conversation with the Microsoft team started in November 2024, with a message from my former skip-level, who was now running a broader organization and wanted to talk about the Copilot work.

item.title: The authentication problem in a virtual room
item.description: When you put on a VR headset, the familiar anchor points disappear.

item.title: What it is like to be an IC again
item.description: The first week of code I wrote at Meta was an authentication middleware layer in Kotlin, and I wrote it alone, on a MacBook Pro that Meta had shipped to the house, sitting at the desk in the home office at about eleven in the morning on a Thursday in February 2024.

item.title: The difference between Microsoft and Meta
item.description: The thing you notice first at Meta, coming from Microsoft, is the pace of the document culture.

item.title: The first Copilot feature nobody saw
item.description: We shipped a voice assistant into Outlook in 2021 that almost no one used.

item.title: Why I left Microsoft for Meta
item.description: The honest version of why I left Microsoft in late 2023 is not the version I gave in exit interviews.

item.title: What enterprise software is actually like to build
item.description: Enterprise feedback doesn't come to you directly. It comes filtered through your PMs, who heard it from a partner, who heard it from a customer's IT department, three meetings removed from the actual problem.

item.title: Cross-team dependencies
item.description: In the spring of 2023, there was a team whose timeline controlled ours.

item.title: Writing a performance review
item.description: The Microsoft performance cycle ends in August, and for the three years I was a manager, I started writing reviews in late July, in the hour before the family calls and after the evening's meetings.

item.title: The ChatGPT moment from inside Microsoft
item.description: ChatGPT launched on a Wednesday in November 2022, and by Friday my calendar had three new recurring meetings on it.

item.title: What I got wrong about being a manager the first time
item.description: The first team I managed had four people on it.

item.title: Being inside a company when it decides to change direction
item.description: In late 2022 we were building a feature that the company was about to rename, reframe, and put on the front page of everything.

item.title: The product that almost shipped
item.description: We called it Compose, though that was an internal name and it never got an external one, because it never shipped.

item.title: The year I stopped writing code
item.description: Somewhere in my second month as a manager, I opened my laptop on a Sunday morning with the intention of writing a small script.

item.title: Shadowing my manager
item.description: From February to September of 2021, I sat in on my manager Jim's one-on-ones with the engineers on his team.

item.title: The first team meeting as a manager
item.description: On September 22, 2021, I ran my first team meeting as the official manager of the Outlook Assistant mobile experiences team.

item.title: The first hire
item.description: His name was Matt, and he was a senior engineer at a mid-sized company in Seattle who had been referred to me by someone on the team.
